question: Explain the fundamental purpose of an encoder in digital circuits and describe a common, real-world application where its function is critical.
expectedPoints: An encoder converts information from one format to another, specifically from 2^N inputs to an N-bit binary code.
It asserts an N-bit output code corresponding to the single active input line.
A common application is in computer keyboards, where pressing a key (an input) is encoded into an ASCII or scan code (a binary output) for the computer to process.

question: Design the truth table and derive the simplified Boolean expressions for a 4-to-2 line priority encoder. Assume the inputs are labeled D3, D2, D1, D0 (where D3 has the highest priority) and the outputs are A1 and A0. Explain how priority is handled in your design.
expectedPoints: The truth table must show that when multiple inputs are active (e.g., D1=1 and D0=1), the output corresponds to the input with the highest priority (D1 in this case).
The simplified Boolean expressions are: A1 = D3 + D2'D2 and A0 = D3 + D2'D1.
The design handles priority by making the output depend only on the highest-order active input, ignoring all lower-priority active inputs.

question: Compare and contrast a simple 4-to-2 line encoder with a 4-to-2 line priority encoder. Identify a key limitation of the simple encoder and explain in detail how the priority encoder's design overcomes this limitation. Include the role of the 'Valid Output' (V) bit in your answer.
expectedPoints: A simple encoder assumes only one input is active at a time. If multiple inputs are active, its output becomes undefined or erroneous.
A priority encoder introduces a hierarchy to inputs, ensuring a valid output is always generated based on the highest-priority active input, thus overcoming the multiple-active-inputs limitation.

The 'Valid Output' (V) bit is a crucial addition; it is asserted only when at least one input is active, distinguishing a valid code (e.g., for input D0) from the default output state (which is also 00 when no inputs are active).
This comparison highlights that priority encoders are essential for real-world systems where multiple input requests can occur simultaneously, such as in interrupt controllers.
